segments.s330-g57: به introduces the indirect object ('to/for'); بیژن is the addressee (proper name). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت. گفت here is the simple past 'he said' (narrative past) and the following noun phrase کین یادگار functions as the quoted object: کین (an archaic demonstrative 'this' in poetic usage) + یادگار 'memento'.
segments.s330-g58: همی is an older/perfective/habitual adverb used here with an imperative sense ('ever/always' or continuous instruction). دار is an imperative 'hold/keep' (i.e. 'keep it'). و links two commands. جز introduces an exception/restriction ('except/only'), governing the following noun phrase تخم نیکی ('seed of goodness' — a metaphorical 'good seed' or good deed). مکار here functions as an admonition tied to the verb phrase — meaning roughly 'do not (do anything else)' or 'do nothing but' in context; the clause thus issues a prohibition/limitation: keep it and do nothing except sow goodness.
segments.s330-g49: A noun-list introduced by the preposition از (from/of). Word order is simply coordinate nouns joined by و. ایوان and خرگاه and پرده‌سرای are locations/rooms being enumerated; از here marks the source or part of the possessions.
segments.s330-g50: A parallel noun-list introduced by همان ('the same' / 'that very'), repeating items taken from the places named before. The three nouns are coordinated by و and enumerate tangible objects (tent, manger, beast).
segments.s330-g51: Poetic/archaic word order: the verb داد (gave) appears before the subject شاه (the king), which comes at the end. کاووس را is marked by the object marker را and therefore stands grammatically as a definite object; فریبرز appears without a preposition and functions as the person involved (recipient or the item named) according to context. Overall the clause means 'the king gave Fereborz (to) Kaavus' or 'the king gave to Kaavus Fereborz' with the precise role clarified by the following list of what was given.
segments.s330-g52: A list of the gifts: بسی here is an adverb/quantifier meaning 'many' or 'a great deal'; جوشن is 'armor/coat of mail'; ترگ and رومی کلاه are coordinated items (a rag/garment and a Roman-style helmet). The nouns are bare and coordinated by و.
segments.s330-g53: یکـی is the numeral/indefinite 'one' or 'a certain'; طوق is 'necklace/collar'; روشن‌تر is the comparative form ('brighter/more luminous') formed from روشن + -تر; از introduces the standard of comparison ('than'); مشتری here is the planet Jupiter used as a poetic standard of brightness.
segments.s330-g54: ز is the poetic/prepositional form of از meaning 'of/from' and introduces the material or source; یاقوت رخشان is a noun phrase 'radiant ruby' (yāqūt = ruby, رخشان = shining/radiant) modifying انگشتری; دو انگشتری gives the quantity 'two rings'. The adjective follows the noun it modifies in the phrase یاقوت رخشان before انگشتری.
segments.s330-g55: نبشته is a noun meaning 'inscription' or 'that which is written' and is placed at the clause head; بر او = 'upon it' (بر = on/upon, او = it/he/she referring to the object described); نام شاه جهان is the phrase 'the name "King of the World"' (نام = name; شاه جهان = king of the world) in apposition — the inscription is the name.
segments.s330-g56: که is a relative pronoun ('which/that') linking to نام; اندر جهان = 'in the world' (اندر = in/within); آن refers back to the named title; نبودی is the past negative form (ن + بودی) meaning 'was not'; نهان is the adjective 'hidden'. The clause therefore states that the name was not hidden in the world.
segments.s330-g41: This is an adverbial prepositional phrase introduced by the poetic preposition ز (here 'from/of' or marking source/setting). اسبان (horses) is the noun, followed by the destination phrase به جایی (to a place). که بودش یله is a relative clause modifying جایی: که = 'where/which', بودش is the archaic enclitic past 'was' (بود + ـش referring back to the place/horses), and یله is an attributive adjective 'loose/slack' describing the tether/status there.
segments.s330-g42: Main clause: a benefactive/goal phrase به طوس سپهبد ('to Tūs, the spahbed') precedes the verb phrase سپردش گله. سپردش is a contracted past-tense verb + enclitic object (سپرد + ـش = 'he/they entrusted it/him'), and گله ('herd') is the direct object. Word order is verb-final in this poetic line, with the indirect/recipient placed before the verb.
segments.s330-g43: Simple clause: همه باغ و گلشن 'all the gardens and groves' is the compound subject/accusative phrase, followed by the prepositional goal به گودرز 'to Goudarz' and the past verb داد 'gave'. The order places the recipients and theme before the verb, typical of Persian poetic narration.
segments.s330-g44: This segment specifies extent: به گیتی 'to/the world' followed by ز مرزی 'from the frontier' and the relative clause که آمدش یاد 'that came to his memory/that he remembered'. آمدش is 'came to him' (آمد + ـش), and یاد is used as 'memory/remembrance' describing the frontier that he recalled—together indicating the territorial bounds he remembered.
segments.s330-g45: Nominal phrase: سلیح تنش 'the weapons/arms of his body' (سلیح = arms, تن + ش = 'his body') followed by the quantifier هرچه 'whatever' and the locative prepositional phrase در گنج 'in the treasury'. بود is the past copula 'was', completing the relative description 'that were in the treasury.'
segments.s330-g46: Explanatory clause introduced by که 'for/that': او را is a dative/experiencer construction 'for him' (او + را for emphasis/indirect object), and the fused expression بدان خواسته functions as 'in that desire/for that seeking' (ب + دان forming بدان). خواسته is the noun/participial sense 'desire/seeking' and رنج بود 'was hardship' uses بود as the copula: literally 'that seeking was a hardship for him.'
segments.s330-g47: Verb phrase with adverbial modifier: سپردند 'they handed/entrusted' is past plural, modified by یکسر 'entirely/wholly', followed by the beneficiary phrase به گیو دلیر 'to Giv the brave'. The order places the adverb between verb and indirect object in this poetic line but the sense is straightforward 'entrusted entirely to Giv.'
segments.s330-g48: Temporal clause: بدانگه 'at that time/thereupon' introduces the subordinate clause که خسرو شد از گنج سیر 'when the king became sated with (his) treasure'. شد is the past of شدن 'became', and the predicate از گنج سیر uses سیر as an adjective/noun meaning 'sated/satisfaction' (i.e., 'satisfied with the treasure').
segments.s330-g9: که is the conjunction 'that' linking this clause to the previous thought. هر گنج را places the definite/particular object (گنج) before the verb using the object marker را; روزی is a temporal noun 'one day / a day comes' introducing a destined time. آگندنیست is a poetic/archaic verbal phrase meaning roughly 'is gathered / becomes filled' (the -نیست here is part of the verbal form in classical style rather than a simple negation).
segments.s330-g10: به سختی is an adverbial phrase 'with difficulty' (به + noun giving an instrumental/means sense). و links the two parallel predications; روزی again marks 'another day / a day comes' and پراگندنیست is the parallel poetic verb meaning 'is scattered/dispensed' (an archaic formation mirroring آگندنیست).
segments.s330-g11: نگه کن is an imperative compound 'look!' where نگه (gaze/watch) combines with the imperative کن. رباطی is the direct object 'a caravanserai/inn' that follows the imperative. که ویران بود is a relative clause 'which/that was ruined' (که introducing the clause, ویران 'ruined', بود past copula).
segments.s330-g12: یکی کان functions as 'one (of them) / a certain one' with کان an archaic particle used in classical Persian to point back to a thing ('one that'). به نزدیک ایران is a locative phrase 'near Iran' (به + نزدیک = 'at/near'), and بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s330-g13: دگر 'another / other' introduces a new example; آبگیری here names a water-works/irrigation-pond or reservoir. که introduces the relative clause and باشد is the classical/polite third-person present-subjunctive/copular form often used in poetry ('is/was' in sense of 'lies'), followed by خراب 'ruined'. The sequence is a nominal + relative clause describing its ruined state.
segments.s330-g14: از ایران وز رنج افراسیاب is a prepositional/agentive phrase giving cause/place: از ایران 'from/in Iran' and وز is a contracted poetic form of و + ز meaning 'and from', so وز رنج افراسیاب reads 'and from the hardship/suffering of Afrasiab' (i.e. ruined by Afrasiab's ravages). Note this is a compressed, poetic word-order and orthography.
segments.s330-g15: دگر 'other' introduces another category; کودکانی is the plural 'children' (indefinite/plural form). که introduces the relative clause and بی‌مادرند is the predicate 'are motherless' formed with the prefix بی- 'without' + مادر 'mother' + the plural copula -ند (classical present).
segments.s330-g16: زنانی 'women' begins the parallel clause; که introduces 'who/that', then بی شوی and بی‌چادرند are two coordinated predicative phrases using بی- 'without': بی شوی 'without a husband' (an older/formal expression) and بی‌چادرند 'are without a veil' (چادر 'veil' + plural copula). The conjunction و links the two 'without' predicates.
segments.s330-g33: دگر here means “another/another one” and precedes the noun it modifies. گنج‌کش is a compound in which کش is an agentive/attributive element attached to گنج, giving the sense “treasure-bearing” or “rich in treasure.” خواندندی is an archaic/verbal form (from خواندن) used here in a participial or verbal-adjunct function meaning roughly “called/summoned” (poetic usage); the whole phrase modifies/introduces عروس (“bride”). Word order (modifier + noun) is typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s330-g34: که begins a relative clause modifying عروس (“the bride who/whom ...”). آگند is an adjectival/predicative word meaning “filled/replete (with),” and here it governs the following agent/subject phrase کاووس — i.e. ‘that Kavus had filled.’ در شهر طوس is a locative phrase (“in the city of Tus”) attached to that verb. The clause therefore specifies the bride as one associated with treasures Kavus had filled the city of Tus with.
segments.s330-g35: به گودرز is an indirect-object/prepositional phrase “to Goudarz.” فرمود is a past-tense verb “he said/commanded” (3rd person). کان here refers to the treasure/ore/item in question and را is the definite-object marker attached to it; ببخش is an imperative/causative meaning “give/grant (away).” The ordering shows the reported command: he told Goudarz (to) give that (کان) away.
segments.s330-g36: This segment lists the recipients, each introduced by به (“to”) for clarity/emphasis: به زال و به گیو و خداوند رخش = “to Zal and to Giv and to the lord (master) of Rakhsh.” The repetition of به before each name is a stylistic/device to mark each beneficiary. خداوند here functions as an honorific/‘lord/master’ governing رخش (“Rakhsh,” the steed).
segments.s330-g37: همه is the universal determiner “all.” جامه‌های تنش is a genitive construction: جامه‌های (“garments of/—s”) + تن (body) + ـش (his), i.e. “all the garments of his body” = “all his garments.” برشمرد is a past-tense verb meaning “he enumerated/counted.” The verb takes that noun phrase as its object; subject is implied from context.
segments.s330-g38: نگه کرد is a compound verbal expression meaning “he kept/kept safe” (نگه + کرد). یکسر is an adverb meaning “entirely/wholly” and here modifies سپرد. به رستم سپرد is a prepositional-object + past verb “he entrusted/handed over to Rostam.” The sequence shows two actions: he kept (them) and then wholly entrusted them to Rostam.
segments.s330-g39: همان is a demonstrative/identifying word “the same” or “that same,” used to repeat/list items previously mentioned. یاره is a noun (here part of an inventory) and is presented in apposition; طوق کنداوران is a genitive construction where طوق (“collar/neck-ring”) is possessed by کنداوران (a plural/agentive noun). The structure is a simple list: ‘the same X and the collar of Y.’
segments.s330-g40: Again همان marks repetition (“the same”), followed by جوشن (“jowshan,” armor/ cuirass) and و (and). گرزهای is the plural-construct ‘maces of/—s’ and گران is an adjective meaning here “heavy/weighty” (in the context of weapons). The phrase is another itemized pair: the same cuirass and heavy maces.
segments.s330-g1: به هشتم نشست از بر گاه شاه is a compact, archaic word order: به هشتم (into/to the eighth place) + نشست (past tense 'sat'). نشَست is the past-root verb 'to sit' with no explicit subject in the segment (subject is supplied by the larger context). از بر گاه شاه literally reads 'from/of the king's court/throne' but here functions idiomatically to mark position 'before/at the king's throne' — note the classical split بر گاه (برگاه) for 'throne/royal place.'
segments.s330-g2: This is a nominal list describing what accompanied him: اَبی modifies یاره (an adjective before its noun), then a coordinating و separates items (گرز 'mace') and another و links زرین کلاه ('golden helmet'). The list is telegraphic (no copula); these are possessions or attendants/items associated with the sitter.
segments.s330-g3: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'); آمدش is آمد + enclitic ش ("it came to him"), and رفتن is the infinitive/noun 'going' (here 'the going into ...'). به تنگی is a prepositional phrase 'into narrowness/need' (tangī = hardship), with فراز as a short adverbial/poetic intensifier. The whole phrase means roughly 'when the coming of going into hardship befell him.'
segments.s330-g4: یکی گنج را is the object phrase 'one (a certain) treasure' with را marking definiteness; درگشادند is a past plural verbal form ('they opened' or 'they unbarred') built from در + گشادن, and باز here functions as an adverb meaning 'again' (or 'open' in some contexts) — together: 'they opened one treasure again.' Word order (object before verb) is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s330-g5: چو again introduces a temporal clause ('when'); بگشاد is an archaic past form 'he opened' (بـِ + گشاد), followed by آن گنج آباد را — 'that prosperous/filled treasure' with را marking it as a definite object. Note the verb appears before the object here (poetic inversion), but the meaning is 'when he opened that treasure.'
segments.s330-g6: وصی کرد is 'he made/appointed (someone) wasī' — وصی is the noun 'legatee/successor' and کرد is the past of 'to make/do.' The following names give who was appointed and of whom: گودرز is the person made wasī, and کشواد را marks Keshvad with را as the definite patient/beneficiary. Classical Persian allows this somewhat flexible ordering: 'he made Goudarz the wasī of Keshvad.'
segments.s330-g7: بدو گفت places the indirect object first: بدو 'to him' + گفت 'he said' (introducing direct speech), followed by the imperative بنگر 'look!' بنگر is the singular imperative of نگرستن (behold/look), and به کار جهان ('at the affairs of the world') is the prepositional object of بنگر.
segments.s330-g8: This is a balanced correlative: چه ... و چه ... functions like 'whether/whatsoever ... and ...' but here better rendered 'both ... and ...'. در آشکار = 'in what is open/apparent' and اندر نهان = 'and in what is hidden/secret.' The parallel prepositions (در / اندر) and adjectives (آشکار / نهان) are a standard poetic pair.
segments.s330-g25: دگر هر کجا = 'elsewhere / wherever' (دگر + هر کجا). رسم آتشکدست is a nominal phrase meaning 'the institution/establishment (rism) of a fire‑temple' (آتشکده + est); the copula is poetic/contracted. The whole segment sets up a location/general condition: 'in other places wherever there was a fire‑temple.'
segments.s330-g26: که introduces an explanatory/cause clause ('because'). بی‌هیربد is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'without a hirbod' (hirbod = Zoroastrian priest). جای is the affected thing 'the place', and ویران شدست is a past/resultative verbal phrase ('has become ruined') using the archaic/poetic copular/past form شدست (شد + است).
segments.s330-g27: سه دیگر = 'three others'. کسی کاو (کَسو/کسی که او) is an old/poetic way to introduce 'one who / someone who' (کاو functions as an archaic relative 'who'), and ز تن بازماند literally 'remained from the body' meaning 'survived' or 'remained alive'. Word order places the quantifier and the relative phrase up front.
segments.s330-g28: به‌روز جوانی is a temporal phrase 'in the days/time of youth' (به‌روز = 'on the day / at the time of', جوانی = 'youth'). درم برفشاند is a simple past clause 'he scattered dirhams' (برفشاند = past 'scattered/strewn', here used of giving out money).
segments.s330-g29: دگر چاهساری = 'another well‑field/area of wells'. که introduces the relative clause; بی‌آب گشت = 'became waterless/dry' with گشت as the past of 'become/turn into'. The clause describes the state the well‑field fell into.
segments.s330-g30: فراوان modifies the quantity of time ('many' / 'plentifully'). بر او سالیان برگذشت is a common construction meaning 'years passed over it' (بر = 'over/on', او = 'it', سالیان = 'years', برگذشت = 'passed by'). The phrase says that many years passed over that (dry) well‑field.
segments.s330-g31: بدین (here written as ب + دین) functions idiomatically as 'with this' or 'by this means' (ب = به/‘to/with’); گنج = 'treasure' is the object/instrument. بادآور آباد کن is an imperative: آباد کن 'make prosperous/restore', with بادآور serving as a descriptive/causal element ('that brings' / 'bringing (prosperity)') modifying what is to be made آباد. کن is the imperative 'do/make'.
segments.s330-g32: درم خوار کن is an imperative clause meaning 'make the dirham despised/cheap' (درم = 'dirham/coin', خوار used predicatively 'despised/worthless', plus imperative کن 'make'). مرگ را یاد کن is another imperative 'remember death' where را is the definite object marker and یاد کن = 'do remembrance' i.e. 'remember'.
segments.s330-g17: دگر = “another” (not ‘again’) placed before a noun to mark a different person; آن کش is a compound where کش is the agentive element ‘-kesh/ -kash’ (‘one who..’), so آن کش = ‘that/that kind of person’; آید is the simple present/future sense ‘comes/will come’; به چیزی نیاز = a prepositional phrase ‘in need of something’ (به + چیز + نیاز). The whole clause = “Another such person comes in need of something.”
segments.s330-g18: ز هر کس = ‘from every person’ (ز is the poetic form of از); همی دارد is the habitual/present construction (همی adds a continuous/habitual flavour) + دارد ‘has/keeps’; آن رنج راز is a poetic inversion meaning ‘that sorrow’s secret’ or ‘that sorrow as a secret’ (رنج = suffering, راز = secret). Together: ‘He keeps that sorrow secret from everyone.’
segments.s330-g19: بر ایشان = ‘toward/for them’ (directing the action to those people); در گنج بسته = ‘closed in the treasure’ (بسته = shut/closed); مدار here is an archaic/prohibitive sense (‘do not make/keep’) — the phrase functions as an admonition: ‘do not keep (it) closed to them.’
segments.s330-g20: ببخش is the imperative of بخشیدن ‘give/forgive’ (here ‘give’ as alms or grant); و = ‘and’; بترس is the imperative ‘fear/be afraid’; از governs the feared object; بد روزگار = ‘bad times/ill fortune.’ The clause means ‘Give (to them) and fear ill days (i.e. be wary of misfortune).’
segments.s330-g21: دگر = ‘another’; گنج کش is a compound agentive ‘treasure‑seeker/treasure‑drawer’ (گنج + کش); نام = ‘name’ (or ‘called’); بادآورست is an adjectival/poetic word meaning ‘wind‑borne’ or ‘vain/empty’ (with the copular sense carried in the -ست ending). So the phrase says there is another treasure (or treasure‑seeker) ‘whose name is vainglorious/empty.’
segments.s330-g22: پر از = ‘full of’ (پر used adjectivally with از); then a coordinate list افسر و زیور و گوهر; the final گوهرست contains the copular -ست so the whole predicate reads as ‘is full of helmets and jewelry and gems.’ In poetry the copula is often attached to the last item.
segments.s330-g23: نگه کن is the imperative ‘look!/behold!’ formed by noun نگه ‘a look/watch’ + the light verb/imperative کن; به introduces the place ‘to/at’; شهری (here used as the noun ‘a city’) + که = relative ‘that’ + ویران شدست = ‘has become ruined’ (شدست = poetic form of شد + است). So: ‘Look at a city that has become ruined.’
segments.s330-g24: کنام = ‘bosom/lair/haunt’ (a poetic locative: the place where something rests); پلنگان و شیران = ‘leopards and lions’; شدست = ‘it has become’ (poetic past/resultative). The clause = ‘It has become the haunt/lair of leopards and lions.’

sentences.s330-s29: He said to Bijan: "Keep this memento always, and do nothing but sow the seed of goodness."
sentences.s330-s25: From the iwan and the stables and the curtain-chamber, the same tent, trough, and beast of burden.
sentences.s330-s26: The king gave Fereborz (to) Kaavus many cuirasses, rags, and a Roman helmet.
sentences.s330-s27: One necklace brighter than Jupiter, and two radiant ruby rings.
sentences.s330-s28: On it was written the name 'King of the World,' which in the world was not hidden.
sentences.s330-s21: He entrusted the herd of horses to the spahbed of Tūs, leaving them at a place where their tethers were slack.
sentences.s330-s22: He gave all the gardens and groves to Goudarz, and the domain as far as the frontier that came to his mind.
sentences.s330-s23: He gave all the arms of his person that were in the treasury, for seeking them had been a hardship for him.
sentences.s330-s24: They handed everything wholly to Giv the brave, at that moment when the king had become sated with treasure.
sentences.s330-s5: For every treasure there comes a day when it is gathered with hardship, and a day when it is scattered.
sentences.s330-s6: Look at a caravanserai that was ruined.
sentences.s330-s7: Another was an irrigation-pond which lay ruined, in Iran and from the hardship of Afrasiab.
sentences.s330-s8: Others were children without mothers, and women without husbands and without veils.
sentences.s330-s17: They brought another bride, laden with treasures that Kavus had amassed in the city of Tus.
sentences.s330-s18: He told Goudarz, “Give that treasure away to Zal and to Giv and to the master (lord) of Rakhsh.”
sentences.s330-s19: He counted all his garments; he kept them and handed everything over to Rostam.
sentences.s330-s20: The same yāreh and the collar of the kandavaran; the same cuirass and heavy maces.
sentences.s330-s1: He sat in the eighth place before the king's throne, (with) a blue attendant, a mace, and a golden helmet.
sentences.s330-s2: When the time came for him to go into hardship, they opened one treasure again.
sentences.s330-s3: When he opened that prosperous treasure, he appointed Goudarz as the wasī (legatee/executor) of Keshvad.
sentences.s330-s4: He said to him, 'Look at the affairs of the world, both what is apparent and what is hidden.'
sentences.s330-s13: Elsewhere, wherever there stood a fire‑temple, because it lacked a hirbod (priest) the place has become ruined.
sentences.s330-s14: Three others: one who remained alive — in the days of his youth he scattered dirhams.
sentences.s330-s15: Another well‑field, which went waterless, endured the passing of many years.
sentences.s330-s16: With this treasure, make (the place) flourish; make dirhams worthless; remember death.
sentences.s330-s9: Another such person will come in need of something; he keeps that sorrow a secret from everyone.
sentences.s330-s10: Do not shut the treasure against them; give (to them) and fear the ill days.
sentences.s330-s11: Another treasure‑seeker (ganzkesh), whose name is vain/empty in fame, was full of helmets, ornaments, and gems.
sentences.s330-s12: Look at a city that has been ruined; it has become the haunt of leopards and lions.
